data_in.description: High-resolution MS/MS spectra in mzML, mzXML, or MGF format

data_out.description: Matrix of low-dimensional hashed feature vectors (one vector per spectrum)

domain_knowledge: Feature hashing is a dimensionality-reduction technique that converts sparse, high-dimensional spectral data (m/z bins) into fixed-size dense or sparse vectors via hash functions, enabling efficient similarity searching without storing the original spectral resolution.
Spectral binning discretizes the continuous m/z range into discrete bins; the choice of bin width affects the trade-off between spectral resolution and computational efficiency.

Low-dimensional hashed vectors enable fast approximate nearest-neighbor search algorithms (e.g., locality-sensitive hashing, LSH) that scale to millions of spectra without exhaustive pairwise comparisons.
Intensity normalization (e.g., TIC, L2-norm) is essential before hashing to ensure comparable vector representations across spectra with different total intensities.

direct_checks: verify that falcon repository (github:bittremieux-lab__falcon or https://github.com/bittremieux/falcon) is accessible and contains implementation code for the feature hashing stage
verify that the hashed feature vector matrix output has file_exists and format_is (numeric matrix, e.g., .npy, .csv, or .h5)
verify that the output matrix row_count_equals the number of input spectra
verify that the output matrix column_count_equals the hash dimension parameter (no canonical answer — depends on implementation choice)
verify script_runs: the binning and hashing transformation executes without runtime errors on a provided test spectrum file
verify that output values are in valid range for hashed feature vectors (e.g., non-negative integers or floats within [0, 1] depending on normalization; parameter-sensitive to hashing scheme)
verify that the implementation uses feature hashing as the mechanism (robust to parameter choices in hash table size and bin width)
expert_review: confirm that the binning strategy (bin width, mass range) is appropriate for typical high-resolution MS/MS spectra and consistent with stated method

confirm that the feature hashing implementation correctly maps m/z–intensity pairs into fixed-dimensional vectors without collision artifacts or information loss that would degrade downstream clustering
confirm that vector normalization or scaling (if applied) is suitable for nearest neighbor distance computations

finding: High-resolution spectra are binned and converted to low-dimensional vectors using feature hashing as the initial transformation step.
inputs: High-resolution MS/MS spectra in mzML, mzXML, or MGF format
methodology_summary: Load high-resolution MS/MS spectra from standard mass spectrometry file formats.
Discretize m/z values into uniform bins and aggregate intensities within each bin.
Normalize binned intensity vectors (e.g., by total ion current or L2-norm).
Apply feature hashing to map binned vectors to fixed-size low-dimensional representations.
Validation: Confirm output is a dense or sparse matrix with one row per input spectrum and consistent vector dimensionality across all spectra.

task_description: Implement the feature-hashing binning stage of falcon: convert high-resolution MS/MS spectra into low-dimensional hashed feature vectors. Output a matrix of hashed spectrum vectors suitable for nearest-neighbor indexing.
task_objective: Implement the feature hashing transformation stage that bins high-resolution MS/MS spectra and outputs low-dimensional hashed feature vectors.

task_outputs.label: Matrix of low-dimensional hashed feature vectors

title: Reconstruct the Spectrum Binning and Feature Hashing Layer of the falcon pipeline

workflow_description: 1. Load high-resolution MS/MS spectra in mzML or mzXML format. 2. Bin spectra across the m/z range and normalize intensities. 3. Apply feature hashing to convert binned spectra into fixed-size low-dimensional vectors. 4. Output the resulting hashed feature matrix in a dense or sparse numerical format (e.g., NumPy array, SciPy sparse matrix, or CSV).
